summary:
Mughal Gardens (c. 1526–1750 AD) were the distinctive formal garden tradition developed under the [[event:103|Mughal emperors]] — based on the Persian chahar bagh ('four gardens') template of a walled enclosure divided by water channels into four quadrants, representing the four rivers of Paradise described in the Quran — that created some of the world's most celebrated garden landscapes and profoundly influenced garden design across South Asia. Mughal garden culture was introduced by [[event:2512|Babur]], who was deeply homesick for the formal gardens of Central Asia and Persia and immediately set about creating gardens in India; his memoirs describe his delight in designing gardens at Agra and other sites. His grandson [[event:2381|Akbar]] elaborated the tradition, and Jahangir was perhaps the most passionate garden enthusiast among the Mughal emperors, spending spring and autumn in Kashmir whose natural landscape he adored.

The greatest Mughal garden achievement is the [[event:2382|Taj Mahal]] complex (1632–1653), where the chahar bagh garden leading to the mausoleum is the most perfectly preserved and most visited example of Mughal garden design. Other masterpieces include the Shalimar Gardens of Lahore and Srinagar (both Jahangir era), and the Nishat Bagh in Kashmir. Mughal garden design influenced later Rajput and British colonial garden traditions in India, and some elements entered the English landscape garden tradition through travelers' accounts. The gardens' emphasis on the control and management of water — fountains, channels, cascades — in an often arid landscape reflected both Persian garden ideals and practical mastery of hydraulic engineering, and created microclimates of shade and mist that made the gardens genuinely comfortable in the Indian climate.
